text:
Dear Margaret,

I have been meaning to write this for some time, and I suppose the right moment is simply whenever you decide to sit down and do it.

Thank you for the years of patience you gave me when I was still learning how to think clearly. I remember the afternoon you pushed my draft back across the table and said, not unkindly, that I had confused motion with progress. That stung, but it was also the most useful sentence anyone said to me that year. You had a gift for finding the exact word that would stick.

What I appreciated most was that you never made me feel small for not knowing something. You seemed to understand that confidence and competence develop together, and that crushing one while trying to build the other is a common mistake that leaves people afraid to try. You never did that. You asked questions instead of delivering verdicts, and you waited for me to find my way to the answer even when I could tell you already knew it.

I also want to acknowledge something that probably seemed small to you at the time. When I was doubting whether I belonged in this field at all, you mentioned offhandedly that you had gone through something similar at my stage and that the doubt was not a sign of weakness but of seriousness. That comment costs you nothing to make, but it meant a great deal to me. I carried it forward.

The work I am doing now has your fingerprints on it in ways I cannot always trace. But I feel it. The habits of rigor, the willingness to sit with a hard problem instead of rushing past it, the belief that good work is worth the difficulty it takes to produce it. Those came from watching you.

With genuine gratitude,

David
